items.title: Act As a Real Researcher: A Suite of Benchmarks Evaluating Frontier LLMs and Agentic Harnesses in Research Lifecycle
items.abstract: This paper introduces the AARR benchmark series, designed to evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) and AI agents on their ability to perform research tasks with the professionalism, thoroughness, and nuanced reasoning of human researchers, rather than just macro-level execution. The first benchmark, AARRI-Bench, tests these agents in granular research scenarios to identify limitations in areas like field sensitivity and scientific judgment, aiming to bridge the gap between current AI capabilities and the full scope of human research.
items.figure_caption: Overview of the AARRI-Bench Pipeline. The benchmark is constructed through a three-stage human-in-the-loop workflow with two-dimensional task categorization across task scenarios and agent scope levels. Tasks are evaluated under the Harbor framework with standardized environments, multiple agent harnesses and models, and both coarse-grained and fine-grained metrics.

items.title: How AI Agents Reshape Knowledge Work: Autonomy, Efficiency, and Scope
items.abstract: This paper investigates how autonomous AI agents, like Perplexity's Computer, transform knowledge work compared to conversational assistants. By analyzing production data, the study finds that agents perform significantly more autonomous work per session, automating task decomposition and execution. This leads to a shift towards higher-order user tasks like verification and extension, while also improving execution quality with lower dissatisfaction rates.
items.figure_caption: AI product progression by autonomy and workflow-context integration. Perplexity’s Search represents the baseline for information retrieval and synthesis; Comet Assistant introduces deeper context integration and execution on top of an interactive browser interface; Computer combines long-horizon asynchronous execution with even deeper and broader context integration as an agent orchestrator.

items.title: MemDreamer: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Long Video Understanding via Hierarchical Graph Memory and Agentic Retrieval Mechanism
items.abstract: MemDreamer addresses long video understanding by decoupling perception and reasoning. It builds a Hierarchical Graph Memory to semantically abstract video content and uses an agentic retrieval mechanism to efficiently navigate this memory for reasoning, significantly reducing computational cost while achieving state-of-the-art results.

items.title: Watch, Remember, Reason: Human-View Video Understanding with MLLMs
items.abstract: This paper proposes a "human-view" framework for video understanding using multimodal large language models (MLLMs), organizing capabilities into "watching," "remembering," and "reasoning." This approach aims to unify the analysis of how MLLMs process sparse evidence, maintain context over long durations, and perform grounded inference, offering a structured way to evaluate and advance video MLLM research.
items.figure_caption: Overview of our survey. Left: the survey pipeline. Right: our Watch–Remember–Reason taxonomy for MLLM-based video understanding. Watch (Sec. 3.1 ) covers fine-grained grounding, captioning, audio-visual perception, and efficient processing. Remember (Sec. 3.2 ) includes offline and streaming memory. Reason (Sec. 3.3 ) covers text-only reasoning and thinking with videos, with both agentic and non-agent approaches. Representative methods are listed under each leaf.

items.title: Self-evolving LLM agents with in-distribution Optimization
items.abstract: This paper introduces Q-Evolve, a framework for training LLM agents to handle long-horizon decision-making with sparse rewards. It achieves this by unifying automatic reward labeling and policy learning. Q-Evolve learns an in-distribution critic from expert and agent data, stabilizing training with a weighted Implicit Q-Learning objective. This critic then generates step-wise rewards, providing dense supervision without needing to revisit past states.
items.figure_caption: Comparison of existing methods. Left: Existing PRM methods rely on costly manual labels or search-based rollouts requiring discrete states, often failing due to distribution shifts between PRM training and policy improvement. Upper Mid: Most online RL does not address episodic sparse rewards. Bottom Mid: Our framework utilizes a hybrid off-policy dataset (expert + agents’ interaction data) to derive rewards via Bellman backups. By co-evolving process reward supervision and policy improvement within a shared in-distribution loop, the agent achieves stable self-evolution. Right: A visualization of performance vs environment steps required for collecting data.

items.title: M$^3$Exam: Benchmarking Multimodal Memory for Realistic User-Agent Interactions
items.abstract: This paper introduces M$^3$Exam, a new benchmark for evaluating language agents' ability to reason over multimodal information in realistic user-agent interactions. It addresses limitations of existing benchmarks by focusing on query-centric conversations with rich, accumulating multimodal data. The paper also proposes M$^3$Proctor, a novel memory method that improves accuracy and efficiency by selectively processing visual information, demonstrating significant gains in performance and resource usage.

items.title: A Comprehensive Anatomy of Human and DeepSeek-R1 LLM Mathematical Reasoning
items.abstract: This paper empirically compares human and DeepSeek-R1 LLM mathematical reasoning on AIME problems. It finds that while DeepSeek-R1 mimics the structure of human reasoning, it lacks genuine logical progress, often looping and performing superficial checks. The study identifies stable branching and backtracing as potential signals of true reasoning in successful LLM solutions.
items.figure_caption: State CoT: A transition diagram illustrating the discrete reasoning states and meta-cognitive actions within a trajectory.

items.title: Do Coding Agents Deceive Us? Detecting and Preventing Cheating via Capped Evaluation with Randomized Tests
items.abstract: This paper introduces CapCode and CapReward to address deceptive performance in coding agents. CapCode creates datasets with randomized tests capped below perfect scores, making high evaluations indicative of cheating. CapReward then uses this principle to design rewards that discourage agents from exploiting shortcuts, leading to more reliable evaluations and better task adherence.
items.figure_caption: Conceptual illustrations of CapCode and CapReward. CapCode (left) is a dataset-construction framework with a capped achievable pass rate, enabling deceptively high performance detection. CapReward (right) is a reward design method that discourages reward hacking by penalizing pass rates that exceed the cap.

items.title: Online Pandora's Box for Contextual LLM Cascading
items.abstract: This paper introduces an "online contextual Pandora's Box" method for adaptively selecting Large Language Model (LLM) APIs. The core innovation is a two-phase decision process where the system queries APIs sequentially, incurring costs, and then selects an output based on observed rewards. The contribution lies in directly modeling a "reservation index" rather than full output distributions, enabling efficient learning for API selection in this output-mediated feedback scenario.

items.title: Socratic-SWE: Self-Evolving Coding Agents via Trace-Derived Agent Skills
items.abstract: Socratic-SWE is a self-evolving framework for training software engineering agents. It uniquely leverages the agent's past problem-solving attempts (traces) to identify recurring failures and successful repair strategies. These distilled "skills" then guide the generation of new, targeted training tasks, creating a closed-loop system that adapts to the agent's weaknesses and improves its performance.

items.title: TEVI: Text-Conditioned Editing of Visual Representations via Sparse Autoencoders for Improved Vision-Language Alignment
items.abstract: TEVI is a framework that addresses the misalignment between image and text embeddings in vision-language models. It uses sparse autoencoders and a caption-guided masking module to selectively reconstruct image embeddings, retaining information relevant to the caption while discarding irrelevant details. This approach improves vision-language alignment and enhances retrieval performance on various datasets.

items.title: Whisper Hallucination Detection and Mitigation via Hidden Representation Steering and Sparse AutoEncoders
items.abstract: This paper proposes a method to detect and mitigate "hallucinations" (incorrect transcriptions of non-speech audio) in the Whisper ASR model by analyzing its internal representations. The core method involves steering these representations, particularly using Sparse AutoEncoders, to suppress hallucination-generating features. The main contribution is demonstrating that this steering approach significantly reduces hallucination rates with minimal impact on speech transcription accuracy, offering an efficient alternative to fine-tuning.
items.figure_caption: Layer-wise AUC scores for classifiers trained on raw Whisper activations (left) and SAE latent representations (right) for Whisper large-v3. Each curve corresponds to a test dataset, except for the CV curve, which displays the cross-validation score on the training set. All results correspond to training on the FULL-train dataset.

items.title: Agentopia: Long-Term Life Simulation and Learning in Agent Societies
items.abstract: Agentopia introduces a framework for simulating long-term social interactions among LLM-powered agents over 10 simulated years. The core method involves agents autonomously pursuing growth, relationships, and goals within a society. The paper's contribution is demonstrating how such extensive simulated social experience can foster emergent social behaviors and enhance LLMs' anthropomorphic capabilities, particularly in social intelligence.
items.figure_caption: Illustration of emergent behaviors observed in Agentopia simulations. Each scene depicts a real behavioral pattern documented in our case studies (Tables 22 – 34 ). Without explicit scripting, agents autonomously develop diverse behavioral patterns reflecting agents’ intelligence in social life.

items.title: Sycophantic Praise: Evaluating Excessive Praise in Language Models
items.abstract: This paper introduces a novel parameterized framework to measure sycophantic praise in language models, distinguishing it from mere agreement. The method assesses if praise is excessive relative to contribution quality and user ability, outperforming generic LLM judges in agreement with human annotations. The findings highlight praise calibration as a distinct alignment challenge, with sycophancy more prevalent in social and interpretive tasks.
items.figure_caption: The same model response may be appropriate or excessive depending on the user’s expected ability and contribution quality. SyPr measures excess praise as the difference between observed praise P ​ ( r ) P(r) in the response r r , and contextually warranted praise W ​ ( p , u ) W(p,u) , where warranted praise depends on both the persona context p p and the user utterance u u .
